column3: 2. The management body of the financial entity shall define, approve, oversee and be responsible for the implementation of all arrangements related to the ICT risk management framework referred to in Article 6(1). For the purposes of the first subparagraph, the management body shall:
(a) bear the ultimate responsibility for managing the financial entity’s ICT risk;
(b) put in place policies that aim to ensure the maintenance of high standards of availability, authenticity, integrity and confidentiality, of data;

(c) set clear roles and responsibilities for all ICT-related functions and establish appropriate governance arrangements to ensure effective and timely communication, cooperation and coordination among those functions;
column7:
• ZPS Guideline External Requirements – [Roles and responsibilities]: Communicates with Business Unit Leads about which legislative acts must they follow.
• ZPS Written Rules and Regulation Policy – [Responsibilities]: The core responsibilities are distinguished between different roles, such as Managing Directors and Department Heads/Leads, who are responsible for reviewing and approving policies, overseeing implementation of strategies, creating and reviewing sfO documents, and resolving any inconsistencies.
• ZPS Process Written Rules and Regulation – [Responsibilities]: The Process requires activities from multiple stakeholders, i.e. actors. Main responsibilities are distinguished between different roles and are as follows: Community of Practice Lead.
• ZPS Process Written Rules and Regulation – [Responsibilities]: The roles of Community of Practice Lead, Document Owner, Document Author, and Document Contributor clearly define the responsibilities for setting clear roles and responsibilities for all ICT-related functions and establishing appropriate governance arrangements to ensure effective communication, cooperation, and coordination among those functions.

column3: (d) bear the overall responsibility for setting and approving the digital operational resilience strategy as referred to in Article 6(8), including the determination of the appropriate risk tolerance level of ICT risk of the financial entity, as referred to in Article 6(8), point (b);

(e) approve, oversee and periodically review the implementation of the financial entity’s ICT business continuity policy and ICT response and recovery plans, referred to, respectively, in Article 11(1) and (3), which may be  dedicated specific policy forming an integral part of the financial entity’s overall business continuity policy andadopted as a response and recovery plan;
(f) approve and periodically review the financial entity’s ICT internal audit plans, ICT audits and material modifications to them;

(g) allocate and periodically review the appropriate budget to fulfil the financial entity’s digital operational resilience needs in respect of all types of resources, including relevant ICT security awareness programmes and  27.12.2022 EN Official Journal of the European Union L 333/29resilience training referred to in Article 13(6), and ICT skills for all staff;
(h) approve and periodically review the financial entity’s policy on arrangements regarding the use of ICT services provided by ICT third-party service providers;

(i) put in place, at corporate level, reporting channels enabling it to be duly informed of the following:
(i) arrangements concluded with ICT third-party service providers on the use of ICT services,
(ii) any relevant planned material changes regarding the ICT third-party service providers,
(iii) the potential impact of such changes on the critical or important functions subject to those arrangements, including a risk analysis summary to assess the impact of those changes, and at least major ICT-related incidents and their impact, as well as response, recovery and corrective measures.

3. Financial entities, other than microenterprises, shall establish a role in order to monitor the arrangements concluded with ICT third-party service providers on the use of ICT services, or shall designate a member of senior management as responsible for overseeing the related risk exposure and relevant documentation.

4. Members of the management body of the financial entity shall actively keep up to date with sufficient knowledge and skills to understand and assess ICT risk and its impact on the operations of the financial entity, including by following specific training on a regular basis, commensurate to the ICT risk being managed.

Financial entities shall ensure that their ICT security policies, information security, and related procedures, protocols, and tools as referred to in Article 9(2) of Regulation (EU) 2022/2554 are embedded in their ICT risk management framework. Financial entities shall establish the ICT security policies, procedures, protocols, and tools that:
ensure the security of networks;
contain safeguards against intrusions and data misuse;
column6:
- Governance process for managing and maintaining authorization concepts.
- Definition and implementation of policies and methodologies that safeguard the organization's information assets.
- Defined process for incident management, monitoring, reporting, and communication of information security events.
- Development and implementation of security measures to comply with relevant regulations and best practices.
- Implementation and management of access, including recording and verifying user identities, granting access permissions, revoking permissions, defining privileged roles, obtaining access request approvals, segregating duties, establishing authorization concepts based on "need-to-know" and "least privilege" principles, implementing time-limited access, and creating centralized Authorization Concept documents for each system.
- Execution of Rapid Security Assessment prior to the project execution phase.
- Regular review of the Control Catalogue (Target Controls) including verification meetings with Asset Owners and development of remediation plans to address identified deficiencies.
- Collection and analysis of quarterly information security incident reports and post-incident reviews to identify potential patterns or trends indicating underlying security control weaknesses.
- Key and Certificates Management including documentation, protection, rotation, and revocation of cryptographic keys.
- Assignment of access rights, including privileged access rights, formal user registration, approval from asset owners, and access criteria establishment.

column6:
- Established a governance process for managing and maintaining authorization concepts (ZPS Authorization Concept Governance Process).
- Appointed an Information Security Officer (ISO) responsible for proposing information security objectives, developing and maintaining the ISMS, creating transparency around strategies, policies, and measures, and ensuring necessary resources are allocated to embed information security throughout the organization (ZPS Policy Information Security).
- Developed a Security Incident & Event Response Procedure for managing security incidents (ZPS Procedure Security Incident & Event Response).
- Implemented an Information Security Policy (ISP) that defines the requirements for information security, including the protection of information and information systems to provide confidentiality, integrity, availability, and authenticity (ZPS Policy Information Security).
- Developed and implemented encryption to ensure the secure transmission and storage of sensitive information (ZPS Guideline Encryption).
- Regularly review the Control Catalogue to verify that selected controls are implemented and operating as intended (ZPS Regular review of the Control Catalogue).
- Defined the requirement to diligently manage ICT as well as serious operational or security incidents, including monitoring, logging, and reporting incidents to the German supervisory authority (BaFin) (ZPS_Guideline_BaFin Incident Reporting_final 2.0).
- Outlined specific protocols, algorithms, and parameters for using cryptographic techniques such as ECDSA key pairs, encryption, and verification processes (ZPS Encryption Procedure).
- Defined responsibilities for gathering and analyzing data on ISMS performance, preparing report drafts, presenting reports to Managing Directors, collaborating with other departments, recommending improvements, reviewing the report, maintaining and updating the reporting procedure, providing timely and accurate data on security incidents, risks, and control effectiveness, attending relevant meetings, providing technical data and insights about IT infrastructure, supporting the Information Security Team with data collection and analysis, reviewing and providing feedback on information security reports, and approving resource allocation and investments for ISMS improvement (ZPS Procedure Information Security Reporting).
- Outlined the processes and procedures for key management, including documentation, protection, rotation, and inventory of cryptographic keys (ZPS Guideline Encryption).
- Outlined the process for incident reporting, including the submission of reports via the BaFin portal by the Second Line of Defense, storage of reports in the BaFin Incident Reporting folder, and the export of submitted data as a PDF for centralized storage (ZPS Procedure BaFin Incident Reporting PP and RM).
- Defined the process for assigning access rights, including privileged access rights, and outlines the need for clear documentation, annual reviews, approval from asset owners, and granting access on a need-to-know basis (ZPS Guideline Access Control).
- Outlined who is responsible for implementing the protection measures defined in the procedure, maintaining the document, and ensuring that measures address the cryptographic threat landscape to satisfy protection needs (ZPS Encryption Procedure).

• ZPS Procedure Security Incident & Event Response  – [Security Incident & Event Response Procedure]: The Security Incident & Event Response Procedure outlines the responsibilities, activities, and communication strategies for managing security incidents, including incident reporting, incident management, notification to third parties, incident correlation, and reporting. It specifies the process for reporting and communicating information security incidents, as well as the governance structure for incident management within Zalando Payments.
• ZPS Policy Information Security – [Information Security Policy]: The Information Security Policy (ISP) at Zalando Payments GmbH defines the requirements for information security, including the protection of information and information systems to provide confidentiality, integrity, availability, and authenticity. It outlines roles and responsibilities for managing information security, sets standards for monitoring security controls, and ensures compliance with laws and regulations.

This includes incidents that affect the security of network and information systems and have a detrimental effect on the availability, authenticity, integrity, or confidentiality of data.
• ZPS Encryption Procedure – [Acceptable Algorithms, Protocols And Parameters]: The excerpt clearly explains how financial entities can ensure the authenticity and integrity of data by using cryptographic techniques such as ECDSA key pairs, encryption, and verification processes. • ZPS Policy Information Security – [Information Security Officer (ISO)]: The ISO is responsible for proposing information security objectives aligned with business objectives, developing and maintaining the ISMS, creating transparency around strategies, policies, and measures, reviewing reports on the ISMS, setting strategic direction for information security initiatives, engaging with stakeholders to define and implement policies, and ensuring necessary resources are allocated for information security.
• ZPS Policy Information Security – [Responsibilities]: The excerpt clearly defines the roles and responsibilities for the development, implementation, and maintenance of ICT security policies, procedures, protocols, and tools, as required by the DORA regulation.

Employees should be treated in the same way. • ZPS Guideline Information Security Audit – [Planning of Information Security Audit
- The ISO approves a program for periodic auditing of information security, including conducting at least one Information Security Audit in the course of one year covering the entire Information Security Management System scope. This aligns with the requirement to identify roles and responsibilities for the development, implementation, and maintenance of ICT security policies, procedures, protocols, and tools.]
• Process for Security Assessment of Projects – [Process for Security Assessment of Projects]: The process outlines the roles and responsibilities for conducting security assessments for ICT projects, including notifying the Infosec team, evaluating the need for a security assessment, completing assessment forms, conducting reviews with stakeholders, implementing additional security measures, and verifying their implementation. It clearly defines the actions to be taken by different stakeholders and the process flow for security assessments in projects.
• ZPS Guideline Access Management – [Access Management Guideline]: The guideline outlines the process of assigning User Accounts to employees and externals, defining privileged roles and responsibilities within a centralized Privileged Access Management Register, obtaining approvals of access requests from authorized Asset Owners, and establishing Authorization Concepts based on the principles of "need-to-know" and "least privilege" to ensure comprehensive coverage and alignment with security policies.
• ZPS_Guideline_BaFin Incident Reporting_final 2.0 – [Classification Process for Major ICT incidents]: The excerpt clearly outlines the process for classifying major ICT incidents, including validation by the second line team (Information Security and IT Compliance) and collaboration with first line teams and management to ensure completeness of information shared.
• ZPS Guideline Information Security Audit – [Appointing Information Security Auditors
Criteria for appointing internal Information Security Auditors are defined, including familiarity with information security, access to ISMS knowledge, and objectivity requirements.]
• ZPS Regular review of the Control Catalogue (Target Controls) – [Responsibilities]: The excerpt clearly defines the roles and responsibilities for the development, implementation, and maintenance of ICT security policies, procedures, protocols, and tools, as required by the DORA requirement. It specifies the responsibilities of the Information Security team, Information Security Officer, Asset Owners, Operational Security, and ICT Risk Management in the process of verifying and testing target controls.
• ZPS Guideline Network Security – [General Requirements]: Controls must be implemented to ensure networks are managed and controlled in a way that protects Zalando Payments’ information. The access control level for networks must follow the required level for protection of information. A process and its associated technical and administrative controls must be implemented to facilitate access, authentication, authorization, and management of network devices and services.
• ZPS Guideline Information Security Reporting – [Additional Reporting to the Manager Director
- The Information Security Officer (ISO) can present ad-hoc reports to the Managing Directors for:
column8:
- Critical information security incidents: Promptly notify the Managing Directors of any major security breaches or threats.
- Emerging threats with potential impact: Share information about new risks and propose mitigation actions.
- Changes in attack vectors: Update the Managing Directors on any shifts in the security landscape due to new projects or initiatives.]
• ZPS Identity Management Procedure – [Responsibilities]: Zalando Payments Business Units are responsible for requesting and approving user accounts and access rights, ensuring that only authorized personnel have the necessary access. Zalando Payments IT Department is responsible for the outsourcing contract and the vendor management, ensuring that the service quality meets business requirements. Zalando SE is responsible for providing and operating the SAP system that underpins identity management, as well as for carrying out HR processes that impact user identities in a manner consistent with Zalando Payments’ regulatory obligations. This clearly outlines the roles and responsibilities for the development, implementation, and maintenance of ICT security policies and procedures as required by the DORA requirement.
• ZPS Guideline Information Security Incident Management – [Response Procedure]: It must define roles for different activities in incident management, monitoring and reporting of information security events and weaknesses; responsibilities of Zalando Payments employees for incident reporting; and responsibilities for security incident response process, security incident correlation, problem management, and communication plan.
• ZPS Guideline Access Control – [Scope]: This guideline applies to all access control systems and processes used to manage access to information assets within Zalando Payments. This includes physical and electronic access to information, as well as access to information systems and applications. This aligns with the requirement to ensure that ICT security policies are aligned to the financial entity’s information security objectives included in the DORA strategy.
• ZPS Guideline Encryption – [Scope]: This guideline applies to employees, staff members, contractors, information and supporting assets. Data Providers must classify all data they are responsible for, following the group Data Classification Policy. The data classification determines the baseline security protections and controls needed to protect data confidentiality, integrity, and availability, including the minimum encryption standards for all institutional data accessed, created, stored, processed, or transmitted.
• ZPS Authorization Concept Governance Process – [Documentation]: The excerpt clearly outlines the processes for monitoring, reviewing, and taking actions related to authorization concepts, which align with the requirement to ensure ICT security policies are aligned to information security objectives and to identify roles and responsibilities for their development, implementation, and maintenance.
• ZPS Policy Trainings and Awareness – [Policy Trainings and Awareness]: The excerpt clearly defines the responsibilities for the development, implementation, and maintenance of ICT security policies, procedures, protocols, and tools by outlining the roles and responsibilities for identifying key topics, delivering training, creating content, monitoring attendance, and updating the inventory of trainings.
• ZPS Encryption Procedure – [Responsibilities]: Engineering Teams are responsible for implementing the protection measures outlined in the Encryption Procedure document and maintaining the entries in the document as defined.
• ZPS Guideline Access Control – [Authentication Mechanisms
Robust authentication mechanisms, such as username/password combinations, certificates, or tokens, must be mandatory to verify a user's identity before granting access to Zalando Payments' information assets. Unique user IDs must be assigned to each user to enable their identification and accountability for their actions. Access control requirements, including complexity, lockout, and password reset policies, must be established for all information assets to safeguard against unauthorized access. Multi-factor authentication (MFA) must be implemented based on the criticality of information assets to provide an additional layer of security. User accounts inactive for a maximum period of 90 days must be disabled after verification, unless otherwise specified and approved.]
• ZPS Identity Management Procedure – [Roles and Responsibilities for ICT Security Policies]: The roles and responsibilities for the development, implementation, and maintenance of ICT security policies, procedures, protocols, and tools are clearly outlined in the document, specifying who is responsible for initiating actions such as temporary deactivation and termination of user accounts.
• ZPS Procedure Information Security Reporting – [Responsibilities]: The document outlines the roles and responsibilities for the development, implementation, and maintenance of ICT security policies, procedures, protocols, and tools, aligning with the DORA requirement.
• ZPS Guideline Mobile Computing – [Security of Mobile devices
This section clearly outlines the roles and responsibilities for ensuring the security of mobile devices, including the requirement for devices to be deployed following hardening procedures and administration software, managed remotely, and supporting secure remote deletion of confidential information. It also mentions the need for detecting, reporting, and mitigating or preventing malicious activities, as well as the precautions users must take to protect their devices.]
• ZPS Procedure Authorization Concept – [Authorization Concept Procedure]: The document defines how access to Zalando Payments systems must be documented and maintained, including the roles and responsibilities for managing access, authentication methodology, system roles and permissions, access governance, reconciliation processes, and segregation of duties concepts. The Security Incident & Event Response Procedure outlines the responsibilities, activities, and communication strategies for incident management, aligning with the requirement to review ICT security policies aligned with the financial entity’s information security objectives at least once a year.]
• ZPS Policy Information Security – [Information Security Officer (ISO)]: The ISO is responsible for proposing information security objectives aligned with business objectives, including legal requirements, regulations, best practices, operational objectives, financial objectives, Zalando Group Strategy, and Zalando Data Strategy, as part of the ISMS. They also engage with stakeholders, including Managing Directors, to define and implement policies and methodologies that safeguard the organization's information assets.
• ZPS Policy Information Security – [Policies, Guidelines, Procedures and Processes
Develop and maintain the ISMS report and present to relevant stakeholders, ensuring alignment with information security objectives included in the DOR strategy.]
• ZPS Guideline Access Management – [Authorization concepts]: A process for reviewing Authorization Concepts must be implemented, occurring at least annually. This review process must ensure that Authorization Concepts remain current and aligned with the organization's security policies.
• ZPS Authorization Concept Governance Process – [Authorization Concept Governance Process]: The document outlines a governance process for managing and maintaining authorization concepts effectively, including establishing a central authority responsible for overseeing the lifecycle, implementing a periodic review process, and creating a management process for authorization concepts. The responsibilities of Information Security and Asset/Business Process Owners are clearly defined, along with the frequency of quarterly identification of new authorization concepts and annual review of existing concepts. The document also details the authorization concept lifecycle phases of Identification, Monitoring, and Review, with specific steps and actions to ensure the integrity of authorization concepts.
• ZPS Guideline Information Security Reporting – [Scope]: The document outlines that the Information Security Reporting Guideline applies to all assets, including information systems and data owned or managed by Zalando Payments, aligning with the requirement to ensure ICT security policies are aligned with the financial entity's information security objectives. The guideline covers various areas of the organization and adheres to industry best practices and regulations, ensuring alignment with the DORA strategy. Additionally, it specifies that regular reporting, including quarterly and annual reports, is conducted to provide timely and insightful information to the Managing Directors.
• ZPS Authorization Concept Governance Process – [Review
Periodic reviews are essential to ensure that authorization concepts remain aligned with business objectives and comply with relevant regulations.]
• ZPS_Guideline_BaFin Incident Reporting_final 2.0 – [Exchange of Information in case other authorities are informed]: BaFin is designated as the central reporting hub for ICT incidents of financial entities in Germany covered by DORA, ensuring that information is immediately forwarded to other relevant authorities, as required by the DORA requirement.
• ZPS Guideline Information Security Reporting – [Management Review]: The systematic review of Information Security Management conducted by the Managing Directors, along with the ISO, verifies that the implemented Information Security Policy and related measures support the respective strategies, fulfilling the requirement for regular review of ICT security policies aligned to the financial entity’s information security objectives in the DORA strategy.
column8:
• Process for Security Assessment of Projects – [Process for Security Assessment of Projects]: The process defines the Rapid Security Assessment executed by the ZPS Information Security team prior to the project execution phase, ensuring that security assessments are conducted for every ICT project as required by the DORA regulation.
• ZPS Guideline Physical Security – [Physical Security Guideline]: The Physical Security guideline complements Information Security Policy and Group House Rules aligning with DORA, PCI DSS, and NIS 2 Directive by introducing a set of best practices that enhance the security posture and protect sensitive information. This aligns the ICT security policies with the financial entity's information security objectives included in the DORA strategy.
• ZPS Guideline Information Security Incident Management – [Response Procedure
This section clearly defines the roles and responsibilities for different activities in incident management, monitoring, and reporting of information security events, as well as the responsibilities for the security incident response process, security incident correlation, problem management, and communication plan. It also mentions the establishment of a communication plan for the timely exchange of information.]
• ZPS Guideline Access Control – [Assignment of access rights]: The process for assigning access rights, including privileged access rights, must be clearly defined, documented, and reviewed annually as required by the DORA requirement.
• ZPS Regular review of the Control Catalogue (Target Controls) – [Target Controls Regular Review Process
- This section clearly outlines the process for regular review of controls, including the frequency of reviews (at least semi-annual), responsibilities of different roles (Information Security, ISO, Asset Owners, Operational Security, SRE), and the steps involved in the review process.]
• ZPS Guideline Mobile Computing – [Version History]: The requirement is explicitly defined in the excerpt under the Version History section, where it states that the document was last reviewed in Nov 2025, aligning with the requirement for regular review at least once a year.
• ZPS Policy Acceptable Use – [Acceptable Use Policy]: The ICT security policies are aligned to the financial entity’s information security objectives included in the DOR strategy by outlining the ethical and responsible utilization of information resources solely for business-related purposes and ensuring the protection of users and the organization from malware, threats, and misuse.
• ZPS Policy Asset Management – [Responsibilities]: The Asset Owner is responsible for organizing a periodical review for accuracy and completeness of assets at least on an annual basis, fulfilling the requirement for regular review as stated in the DORA requirement.
• ZPS Identity Management Procedure – [Monitoring and Review]: This section clearly outlines that ZPS is committed to regularly monitoring and reviewing procedures, including periodic audits, risk assessments, and reviews of security incidents, to ensure effectiveness. The IT department, in collaboration with the HR department, conducts these reviews and reports findings to senior management, aligning the identity management system with business objectives, regulatory obligations, and security requirements.
• ZPS Procedure Authorization Concept – [Regular Reviews]: Authorization concepts must be reviewed at least once within 365 days by the asset owners. This does not affect other obligations to review access in defined intervals. Asset owners are responsible to establish corresponding processes.

column3: 1.   Financial entities shall have a sound, comprehensive and well-documented ICT risk management framework as part of their overall risk management system, which enables them to address ICT risk quickly, efficiently and comprehensively and to ensure a high level of digital operational resilience.

2.   The ICT risk management framework shall include at least strategies, policies, procedures, ICT protocols and tools that are necessary to duly and adequately protect all information assets and ICT assets, including computer software, hardware, servers, as well as to protect all relevant physical components and infrastructures, such as premises, data centres and sensitive designated areas, to ensure that all information assets and ICT assets are adequately protected from risks including damage and unauthorised access or usage.

3. In accordance with their ICT risk management framework, financial entities shall minimise the impact of ICT risk by deploying appropriate strategies, policies, procedures, ICT protocols and tools. They shall provide complete and updated information on ICT risk and on their ICT risk management framework to the competent authorities upon their request.

4.   Financial entities, other than microenterprises, shall assign the responsibility for managing and overseeing ICT risk to a control function and ensure an appropriate level of independence of such control function in order to avoid conflicts of interest. Financial entities shall ensure appropriate segregation and independence of ICT risk management functions, control functions, and internal audit functions, according to the three lines of defence model, or an internal risk management and control model.

5. The ICT risk management framework shall be documented and reviewed at least once a year, or periodically in the case of microenterprises, as well as upon the occurrence of major ICT-related incidents, and following supervisory instructions or conclusions derived from relevant digital operational resilience testing or audit processes. It shall be continuously improved on the basis of lessons derived from implementation and monitoring.

Financial entities shall develop, document and implement policies and procedures concerning ICT risk management that are necessary to ensure the security of networks, enable adequate safeguards against intrusions and data misuse, preserve the availability, authenticity, integrity and confidentiality of data, including cryptographic techniques, and guarantee an accurate and prompt data transmission without major disruptions and undue delay. The policies and procedures concerning ICT risk management shall include all of the following:
the indication of the approval of the risk tolerance level for ICT risk

the procedure and the methodology to conduct the ICT risk assessment, identifying vulnerabilities and threats that affect or may affect the supported business functions, the ICT systems and ICT assets supporting those functions and the quantitative or qualitative indicators to measure impact and likelihood of those vulnerabilities being exploited by threats;

the procedure to identify, implement and document ICT risk treatment measures for
the ICT risk assessed, including the determination of ICT risk treatment measures necessary to bring ICT risk within the risk tolerance levels. The procedure shall ensure the monitoring of the effectiveness of the measures implemented, the assessment of whether the established risk tolerance levels of the financial entity have been attained and that the financial entity takes actions to correct or improve the measures where necessary;

provisions on the monitoring of any changes to the ICT risk and cyber threat landscape, internal and external vulnerabilities and threats and of ICT risk of the financial entity to promptly detect changes that could affect its ICT risk profile;
provisions on a process to ensure that changes to the business strategy and the digital operational resilience strategy of the financial entity, if any, are taken into account.

A report on the review of the ICT risk management framework shall be submitted to the competent authority upon its request. For Format and Content please see Art. 27 RTS-RMF and include the content and formatting requirements in the ICT Risk Management Policy

In order to address and manage ICT risk, financial entities shall use and maintain updated ICT systems, protocols and tools that are 
1) appropriate to the magnitude of operations
2) reliable
3) equipped with sufficient capacity to accurately process the data necessary for the performance of activities and the timely provision of services, and to deal with peak orders, message or transaction volumes, as needed, including where new technology is introduced
4) technologically resilient in order to adequately deal with additional information processing needs as required under stressed market conditions or other adverse situations

column3: 1. As part of the ICT risk management framework, financial entities shall identify, classify and adequately document all ICT supported business functions, roles and responsibilities, the information assets and ICT assets supporting those functions, and their roles and dependencies in relation to ICT risk. Financial entities shall review as needed, and at least yearly, the adequacy of this classification and of any relevant documentation.

2.   Financial entities shall, on a continuous basis, identify all sources of ICT risk, in particular the risk exposure to and from other financial entities, and assess cyber threats and ICT vulnerabilities relevant to their ICT supported business functions, information assets and ICT assets. Financial entities shall review on a regular basis, and at least yearly, the risk scenarios impacting them.

4.   Financial entities shall identify all information assets and ICT assets, including those on remote sites, network resources and hardware equipment, and shall map those considered critical. They shall map the configuration of the information assets and ICT assets and the links and interdependencies between the different information assets and ICT assets.

5.   Financial entities shall identify and document all processes that are dependent on ICT third-party service providers, and shall identify interconnections with ICT third-party service providers that provide services that support critical or important functions.

7.   Financial entities, other than microenterprises, shall on a regular basis, and at least yearly, conduct a specific ICT risk assessment on all legacy ICT systems and, in any case before and after connecting technologies, applications or systems.

7. Financial entities, other than microenterprises, shall monitor relevant technological developments on a continuous basis, also with a view to understanding the possible impact of the deployment of such new technologies on ICT security requirements and digital operational resilience. They shall keep up-to-date with the latest ICT risk management processes, in order to effectively combat current or new forms of cyber-attacks.

3.   Financial entities, other than microenterprises, shall perform a risk assessment upon each major change in the network and information system infrastructure, in the processes or procedures affecting their ICT supported business functions, information assets or ICT assets.

As part of the ICT risk management framework referred to in Article 6(1), financial entities shall:
(c) implement policies that limit the physical or logical access to information assets and ICT assets to what is required for legitimate and approved functions and activities only, and establish to that end a set of policies, procedures and controls that address access rights and ensure a sound administration thereof;

3. Financial entities shall devote sufficient resources and capabilities to monitor user activity, the occurrence of ICT anomalies and ICT-related incidents, in particular cyber-attacks.

1. Financial entities shall have in place capabilities and staff to gather information on vulnerabilities and cyber threats, ICT-related incidents, in particular cyber-attacks, and analyse the impact they are likely to have on their digital operational resilience.

As part of the ICT security procedures financial entities shall develop, document and implement capacity and performance management procedures to identify capacity requirements of their ICT systems and apply resource optimisation and monitoring procedures to maintain and improve the availability of data and ICT systems and efficiency of ICT systems and prevent ICT capacity shortages

The capacity and performance management procedures shall ensure that appropriate measures are taken to cater for the specificities of ICT systems with long or complex  procurement or approval processes or that are resource-intensive

measures to preserve the availability, authenticity, integrity and confidentiality of the data, including a clear desk policy for papers and a clear screen policy for information processing facilities.

measures to preserve the availability, authenticity, integrity and confidentiality of the data, including a clear desk policy for papers and a clear screen policy for information processing facilities.

As part of the ICT security procedures, with a view to ensuring the security of networks and information systems against intrusions and data misuse, in order to preserve the availability, authenticity, integrity and confidentiality of data, financial entities shall develop, document and implement a data and ICT system security procedure. The data and ICT system security procedure shall include all of the following elements related to data and ICT system security

the access restrictions supporting the protection requirements
 for each level of classification

identification of secure configuration baseline for ICT assets that will minimise their
 exposure to cyber threats and measures to verify regularly that these baselines are those that are effectively deployed. The secure configuration baseline shall take into account leading (standard) practices and appropriate techniques
identification of security measures to ensure that only authorised software is installed in ICT systems and endpoint devices;
identification of security measures against malicious codes
identification of security measures to ensure that only authorised data storage media, ICT systems and endpoint devices are used to transfer and store data of the financial entity

requirements to secure the use of portable endpoint devices and private non-portable endpoint devices as follows:
 (i) the use of a management solution to remotely manage the endpoint devices and
 remotely wipe the financial entity’s data;
 (ii) the use of security mechanisms that cannot be modified, removed or bypassed by
 staff members or ICT third-party service providers in an unauthorised manner;
 (iii)the authorisation to use removable data storage devices only where the residual
 ICT risk remains within the financial entity’s risk tolerance level
the process to securely delete data, present on premises or stored externally, that the financial entity no longer needs to collect or to store;
the process to securely dispose or decommission of data storage devices present on premises or stored externally containing confidential information;
the identification and implementation of security measures to prevent data loss and leakage for ICT systems and endpoint devices
the implementation of security measures to ensure that teleworking and the use of private endpoint devices does not adversely impact the ICT security of the financial entity;

1.   For the purpose of assessing preparedness for handling ICT-related incidents, of identifying weaknesses, deficiencies and gaps in digital operational resilience, and of promptly implementing corrective measures, financial entities, other than microenterprises, shall, taking into account the criteria set out in Article 4(2), establish, maintain and review a sound and comprehensive digital operational resilience testing programme as an integral part of the ICT risk-management framework referred to in Article 6.
2.   The digital operational resilience testing programme shall include a range of assessments, tests, methodologies, practices and tools to be applied in accordance with Articles 25 and 26.

3.   When conducting the digital operational resilience testing programme referred to in paragraph 1 of this Article, financial entities, other than microenterprises, shall follow a risk-based approach taking into account the criteria set out in Article 4(2) duly considering the evolving landscape of ICT risk, any specific risks to which the financial entity concerned is or might be exposed, the criticality of information assets and of services provided, as well as any other factor the financial entity deems appropriate.

4.   Financial entities, other than microenterprises, shall ensure that tests are undertaken by independent parties, whether internal or external. Where tests are undertaken by an internal tester, financial entities shall dedicate sufficient resources and ensure that conflicts of interest are avoided throughout the design and execution phases of the test.

5.   Financial entities, other than microenterprises, shall establish procedures and policies to prioritise, classify and remedy all issues revealed throughout the performance of the tests and shall establish internal validation methodologies to ascertain that all identified weaknesses, deficiencies or gaps are fully addressed.

6.   Financial entities, other than microenterprises, shall ensure, at least yearly, that appropriate tests are conducted on all ICT systems and applications supporting critical or important functions.

1.   The digital operational resilience testing programme referred to in Article 24 shall provide, in accordance with the criteria set out in Article 4(2), for the execution of appropriate tests, such as vulnerability assessments and scans, open source analyses, network security assessments, gap analyses, physical security reviews, questionnaires and scanning software solutions, source code reviews where feasible, scenario-based tests, compatibility testing, performance testing, end-to-end testing and penetration testing.

Financial Entities are required to establish a comprehensive policy on encryption and cryptographic controls as part of their ICT security policies, incorporating key elements to effectively manage these security measures. When determining encryption requirements, they should consider data classification and ICT risk assessment results. This policy should also cover the encryption of internal network connections and traffic with external parties, considering data criticality and classification. The policy on encryption and cryptographic controls shall be designed on the basis of the results of approved data classification and ICT risk assessment and shall include all the following elements:
rules for the encryption of data at rest and in transit;

rules for the encryption of data in use, where necessary. Where encryption of data in use is not possible, financial entities shall process data in use in a separated and protected environment or take other equivalent measures that ensure the confidentiality, integrity,authenticity and availability of data;
rules for the encryption of internal network connections and traffic with external parties
provisions for cryptographic key management establishing the correct use, protection and lifecycle of cryptographic keys

Financial entities shall include in the policy on encryption and cryptographic controls criteria to select cryptographic techniques and use practices taking into account leading practices and standards

Financial entities shall include in the policy on encryption and cryptographic controls provisions to, where necessary, on the basis of developments in cryptanalysis, update or change the cryptographic technology to ensure they remain resilient against cyber threats and considering the information resources
Financial entities shall include a requirement in the policy on encryption and cryptographic controls to record the adoption of mitigation and monitoring measures adopted

Financial entities shall lay out in the provisions on cryptographic key management referred to in Article 6(2) point (d), the requirements for managing cryptographic keys through their whole lifecycle, including generating, renewing, storing, backing up, archiving, retrieving, transmitting, retiring, revoking and destroying keys.

Financial entities shall identify and implement controls to protect cryptographic keys through their whole lifecycle against loss, unauthorised access, disclosure and modification.
 The controls shall be designed taking into account the results of the approved data classification and the ICT risk assessment processes.
Financial entities shall develop and implement methods to replace the cryptographic keys in the case of lost, compromised or damaged keys.

Financial entities shall create and maintain a register for all certificates and certificate-storing devices for at least ICT assets supporting critical or important functions. The register  shall be kept up-to-date.
Financial entities shall ensure the prompt renewal of certificates in advance of their expiration.
As part of their human resource or other relevant policies financial entities shall include all of the following ICT security related elements:
identification and assignment of any specific ICT security responsibilities;

4. As part of the ICT risk management framework referred to in Article 6(1), financial entities shall:
(e) implement documented policies, procedures and controls for ICT change management, including changes to software, hardware, firmware components, systems or security parameters, that are based on a risk assessment approach and are an integral part of the financial entity’s overall change management process, in order to ensure that all changes to ICT systems are recorded, tested, assessed, approved, implemented and verified in a controlled manner;

column8:
• Software testing Guideline.docx – [Test documentation
The Test Manager ensures that the involved Test Engineers and Software Tester document the execution of a test plan as part of the software release. Test Engineers ensure that automated tests document their execution during the CI/CD pipeline runs. For any kind of manual test, the Test Manager ensures that the Test Engineers, Software Testers, or Change Request Authors document the test execution. This can happen either in the related tickets, like User Stories or Epics, or in a separate document next to the corresponding test plan. 
This section clearly outlines the process of documenting test execution, which is a key element of verifying that ICT security requirements have been met as per the DORA requirement.]
• Software testing Guideline.docx – [Environments and how to use them]: The excerpt clearly outlines the process of testing software changes in different environments, including lower-level test cases, automated tests in the CI/CD pipeline, integration testing, and user acceptance testing before releasing software into production. This process ensures verification that ICT security requirements have been met as part of the ICT change management procedures.
• Change Management Guideline.docx – [Assessment of Risks]: For each change, it needs to be checked if the protection needs of the assets in scope are altered. The confidentiality, integrity, authenticity, and availability (CIAA) assessment has to be conducted. The author of the change request has to initiate this process with the Operational Security Manager.
Defines responsibilities for reviewing and approving changes to ensure compliance with acceptance criteria, including risk and compliance acceptance criteria.]
• ZPS Guideline Secure Development.docx – [Security in software development lifecycle phases]: Reviews must be performed to verify that security requirements were addressed as per risk appetite defined by the management.
• ZPS Guideline Secure Development.docx – [Identify Security Requirements]: Clearly defined security requirements must be established for all applications, and security requirements must be traceable and verifiable, fulfilling the verification that ICT security requirements have been met as per the DORA requirement.
• Application Development Policy.docx – [Change and Release Approval]: All changes must undergo an approval process after implementation involving review and endorsement by technical and subject matter experts in accordance with the criticality assessment. Approval is contingent upon the successful completion of requirements analysis, risk assessment, implementation, and testing phases.
][Justification]: The excerpt clearly outlines the stages involved in the Change Request process, including the Verification stage where the Change Request is executed and verified to meet the requirements.
• Secure Software Development Lifecycle Procedure.docx – [Security Dashboard]: Security Dashboard provides an overview of detected vulnerabilities, shows their severity and a description of the issue. It enables users to identify vulnerabilities for their deployments which are sorted by severity. It’s a frontend to the vulnzone backend component which contains the scan engines and issue tracking. Security Dashboard does not report all identified vulnerabilities from all security tools but currently focuses on scanning the container images and manually added issues by the Information Security and Operational Security teams. Additionally, it provides SBOM functionality.
• PCI Vendor Evaluations Procedure.docx – [Procedure steps]: The procedure outlines specific steps such as checking the Compliance Tracking Sheet, obtaining PCI DSS AoCs/SAQs, and checking the validity of PCI DSS AoCs to ensure that ICT security requirements have been met for vendors accepting payment cards.
- The ZSE Security Risk and Governance (SRG) team conducts security risk assessments based on the updated security questionnaire and assurance documents provided by the InPro team. This process verifies that ICT security requirements have been met by engaging with the vendor's security team and validating the certification date of the provided assurance documentation.]

column3: 1. For the purposes of adequately protecting ICT systems and with a view to organising response measures, financial entities shall continuously monitor and control the security and functioning of ICT systems and tools and shall minimise the impact of ICT risk on ICT systems through the deployment of appropriate ICT security tools, policies and procedures.

As part of the ICT security policies and procedures, financial entities shall develop, document and implement policies and procedures to manage the ICT operations of ICT assets, with a view to ensuring the security of networks, enabling adequate safeguards against intrusions and data misuse and preserving the availability, authenticity, integrity and confidentiality of data. These policies and procedures shall define how financial entities  operate, monitor, control and restore their ICT assets, including the documentation of ICT operations. The policies and procedures for ICT operations shall include all of the following elements:

The ICT project management policy shall define the elements to ensure the effective management of the ICT projects related to the acquisition, maintenance and, where applicable, development of the financial entity’s ICT systems. The ICT project management policy shall include all of the following elements:

column3: 6. Financial entities shall develop ICT security awareness programmes and digital operational resilience training as compulsory modules in their staff training schemes. Those programmes and training shall be applicable to all employees and to senior management staff, and shall have a level of complexity commensurate to the remit of their functions. Where appropriate, financial entities shall also include ICT third-party service providers in their relevant training schemes in accordance with Article 30(2), point (i).

As part of their control of access management rights, financial entities shall develop, document and implement identity management policies and procedures to ensure the unique identification and authentication of natural persons and systems accessing the financial entities' information to enable assignment of user access rights. These policies and procedures shall include all of the following elements

the assignment of a unique identity corresponding to a unique user account to each staff member of the financial entity or staff of the third-party service providers accessing the information assets and ICT assets of the financial entity;

the maintenance of records of all identity assignments referred to in point (a). These records shall be kept following a reorganisation of the financial entity or after the end of the contractual relationship without prejudice to the retention requirements set out in Union and national law

a lifecycle management process for identities and accounts managing the creation, change, review and update, temporary deactivation and termination of all accounts. Where applicable, financial entities shall deploy automated solutions for the lifecycle identity management process

2. Financial entities shall design, procure and implement ICT security policies, procedures, protocols and tools that aim to ensure the resilience, continuity and availability of ICT systems, in particular for those supporting critical or important functions, and to maintain high standards of availability, authenticity, integrity and confidentiality of data, whether at rest, in use or in transit.

4. As part of the ICT risk management framework referred to in Article 6(1), financial entities shall:
(a) develop and document an information security policy defining rules to protect the availability, authenticity, integrity and confidentiality of data, information assets and ICT assets, including those of their customers, where applicable;

As part of the safeguards against intrusions and data misuse and to preserve the availability, authenticity, integrity and confidentiality of data, financial entities shall develop,document and implement logging procedures, protocols and tools. The logging procedures, protocols and tools shall include all of the following:

the identification of the events to be logged, the retention period of the logs and the measures to secure and handle the log data, considering the purpose for which the logs are created. The retention period shall be defined taking into account the business and information security objectives, the reason for recording the event in the logs and the results of the ICT risk assessment
alignment of the level of detail of the logs with their purpose and usage to enable the effective detection of anomalous activities
measures to protect logging systems and log information against tampering, deletion and unauthorised access at rest, in transit and, where relevant, in use;
measures to detect failure of logging systems;

without prejudice to any applicable regulatory requirements under national or Union law, the synchronisation of the clocks of each of the financial entity’s ICT systems upon a documented reliable reference time source.

4. As part of the ICT risk management framework referred to in Article 6(1), financial entities shall:
(b) following a risk-based approach, establish a sound network and infrastructure management structure using appropriate techniques, methods and protocols that may include implementing automated mechanisms to isolate affected information assets in the event of cyber-attacks;

For the purposes of the first subparagraph, point (b), financial entities shall design the network connection infrastructure in a way that allows it to be instantaneously severed or segmented in order to minimise and prevent contagion, especially for interconnected financial processes.

As part of the safeguards to ensuring the security of networks against intrusions and data misuse and in order to preserve the availability, authenticity, integrity and confidentiality of data financial entities shall develop, document and implement policies, procedures, protocols and tools on network security management, including all of the following elements:

the segregation and segmentation of ICT systems and networks taking into account the criticality or importance of the function they support, the classification established and the overall risk profile of ICT assets using them;
the documentation of all of the financial entity’s network connections and data flows;
the use of a separate and dedicated network for the administration of ICT assets;

the identification and implementation of network access controls to prevent and detect connections to the financial entity's network by any unauthorised device or system, or any endpoint not meeting the financial entity's security requirements

the encryption of network connections passing over corporate networks, public networks, domestic networks, third-party networks and wireless networks, for communication protocols used taking into account the results of the approved data classification and the results of the ICT risk assessment
the design of networks in accordance with ICT security requirements and taking intoaccount leading practices to ensure the confidentiality, integrity and availability of the network
the securing of network traffic between the internal networks and the internet and other external connections;

the identification of the roles and responsibilities and steps for the definition, implementation, approval, change and review of firewall rules and connections filters. Financial entities shall perform the review of firewall rules and connections filters on a regular basis according to the classification and overall risk profile of ICT systems involved. For the ICT systems supporting critical or important functions, the financial entities shall verify the adequacy of the existing firewall rules and connection filters at least every six months
the performance of reviews of the network architecture and of the network security design once a year, and periodically for microenterprises, to identify potential vulnerabilities
the measures to temporarily isolate, where necessary, subnetworks and network components and devices

the implementation of a secure configuration baseline of all network components and hardening the network and network devices according to vendor instructions, to, where applicable, standards and leading practices
the procedures to limit, lock and terminate system and remote sessions after a predefined period of inactivity;

with reference to network services agreements, the identification and definition of ICT and information security measures, service levels and management requirements of all network services, whether these services are provided by an ICT intra-group service provider or by ICT third-party service providers

4. As part of the ICT risk management framework referred to in Article 6(1), financial entities shall:
(f) have appropriate and comprehensive documented policies for patches and updates.

As part of the ICT security procedures, financial entities shall develop, document and implement patch management procedures with a view to ensuring the security of networks and enabling safeguards against intrusions and data misuse in order to preserve the availability, authenticity, integrity and confidentiality of data. The patch management procedure shall:
identify and evaluate available software and hardware patches and updates using automated tools, to the extent possible;
identify emergency procedures for the patching and updating of ICT assets;
test and deploy software and hardware patches and updates
set deadlines for the installation of software and hardware patches and updates and escalation procedures in case the deadline cannot be met.

As part of the safeguards to preserve the availability, authenticity, integrity and confidentiality of data, financial entities shall define, document and implement a physical and environmental security policy, which shall be designed according to the cyber threat landscape, to the classification and to the overall risk profile of ICT assets and information assets that can be accessed. The physical and environmental security policy shall include all of the following:
a reference to the section of the policy on control of access management rights referred

measures to protect the premises, data centres of the financial entity and sensitive designated areas identified by the financial entity where ICT assets and information assets reside from attacks, accidents and from environmental threats and hazards. The measures to protect from environmental threats and hazards shall be commensurate with the importance of the premises, data centres, sensitive designated areas and the criticality of the operations or ICT systems located there;

measures to secure ICT assets, both within and outside the premises of the financial entity, taking into account the results of the ICT risk assessment related to the relevant ICT assets. The physical and environmental security policy shall include measures to provide appropriate protection to unattended ICT assets;

measures to ensure the availability, authenticity, integrity and confidentiality of data information assets and physical access control devices of the financial entity through the appropriate maintenance;

measures to preserve the availability, authenticity, integrity and confidentiality of the data, including a clear desk policy for papers and a clear screen policy for information processing facilities.

As part of the safeguards to preserve the availability, authenticity, integrity and confidentiality of data, financial entities shall develop, document and implement the policies, procedures, protocols and tools to protect information in transit. In particular, financial entities shall ensure all of the following
the availability, authenticity, integrity and confidentiality of data during network transmission, as well as the establishment of procedures to assess compliance with these requirements;
the prevention and detection of data leakage and the secure transfer of information between the financial entity and external parties;

that requirements on confidentiality or non-disclosure arrangements reflecting the financial entity’s needs for the protection of information for both the staff of the financial entity and of third parties are implemented, documented and regularly reviewed.
take into account the results of the approved data classification and the ICT risk assessment processes

column3: As part of the ICT security procedures, financial entities shall develop, document and implement vulnerability management procedures with a view to ensuring the security of networks against intrusions and data misuse in order to preserve the availability, authenticity, integrity and confidentiality of data.The vulnerability management procedures shall:
identify and update relevant and trustworthy information resources to build and maintain awareness about vulnerabilities

ensure the performance of automated vulnerability scanning and assessments on ICT  assets, with the frequency and scope of these activities commensurate to the classification and the overall risk profile of the ICT asset. For the ICT assets supporting critical or important functions it shall be performed at least on a weekly basis;

verify that ICT third-party service providers handle vulnerabilities related to the ICT
 services provided to the financial entity and that they report to the financial entity in a timely manner at least the critical vulnerabilities and statistics and trends. In particular, financial entities shall request that ICT third-party service providers investigate the relevant vulnerabilities, determine the root causes and implement appropriate mitigating actions

track the usage of third-party libraries, including open source, used by ICT services supporting critical or important function, of ICT services developed by the financial entity itself or specifically customised or developed for the financial entity by an ICT third-party service provider. The financial entity, in collaboration with the ICT third-party service provider as appropriate, shall monitor the version and possible updates of the third-party libraries. In case of ready to use (off-the-shelf) ICT assets or components of ICT assets
 acquired and used in the operation of ICT services not supporting critical or important functions, the financial entity shall track to the extent possible the usage of third-party libraries, including open-source ones;
establish procedures for responsible disclosure of vulnerabilities to clients and counterparts as well as to the public, as appropriate;

identify criteria to prioritise the deployment of patches and other mitigation measures to address the vulnerabilities identified. For the purposes of the prioritisation, financial
 entities shall consider the criticality of the vulnerability, the classification and the risk profile of the ICT assets affected by the identified vulnerabilities
monitor and verify the remediation of vulnerabilities;
require the recording of any detected vulnerabilities affecting ICT systems and the monitoring of their resolution.

2. Financial entities shall set up backup systems that can be activated in accordance with the backup policies and procedures, as well as restoration and recovery procedures and methods. The activation of backup systems shall not jeopardise the security of the network and information systems or the availability, authenticity, integrity or confidentiality of data. Testing of the backup procedures and restoration and recovery procedures and methods shall be undertaken periodically.

3. When restoring backup data using own systems, financial entities shall use ICT systems that are physically and logically segregated from the source ICT system. The ICT systems shall be securely protected from any unauthorised access or ICT corruption and allow for the timely restoration of services making use of data and system backups as necessary.
4. Financial entities, other than microenterprises, shall maintain redundant ICT capacities equipped with resources, capabilities and functions that are adequate to ensure business needs.

6. In determining the recovery time and recovery point objectives for each function, financial entities shall take into account whether it is a critical or important function and the potential overall impact on market efficiency. Such time objectives shall ensure that, in extreme scenarios, the agreed service levels are met.

5. As part of the overall business continuity policy, financial entities shall conduct a business impact analysis (BIA) of their exposures to severe business disruptions. Under the BIA, financial entities shall assess the potential impact of severe business disruptions by means of quantitative and qualitative criteria, using internal and external data and scenario analysis, as appropriate. The BIA shall consider the criticality of identified and mapped business functions, support processes, third-party dependencies and information assets, and their interdependencies. Financial entities shall ensure that ICT assets and ICT services are designed and used in full alignment with the BIA, in particular with regard to adequately ensuring the redundancy of all critical components.

7. Financial entities, other than microenterprises, shall have a crisis management function, which, in the event of activation of their ICT business continuity plans or ICT response and recovery plans, shall, inter alia, set out clear procedures to manage internal and external crisis communications in accordance with Article 14.

8. Financial entities shall keep readily accessible records of activities before and during disruption events when their ICT business continuity plans and ICT response and recovery plans are activated.

6. As part of their comprehensive ICT risk management, financial entities shall:
(a) test the ICT business continuity plans and the ICT response and recovery plans in relation to ICT systems supporting all functions at least yearly, as well as in the event of any substantive changes to ICT systems supporting critical or important functions;

6. As part of their comprehensive ICT risk management, financial entities shall:
(b) test the crisis communication plans established in accordance with Article 14.

6. For the purposes of the first subparagraph, point (a), financial entities, other than microenterprises, shall include in the testing plans scenarios of cyber-attacks and switchovers between the primary ICT infrastructure and the redundant capacity, backups and redundant facilities necessary to meet the obligations set out in Article 12.

Financial entities shall regularly review their ICT business continuity policy and ICT response and recovery plans, taking into account the results of tests carried out in accordance with the first subparagraph and recommendations stemming from audit checks or supervisory reviews.

Financial entities shall regularly review their ICT business continuity policy and ICT response and recovery plans, taking into account the results of tests carried out in accordance with the first subparagraph and recommendations stemming from audit checks or supervisory reviews.

Financial entities shall test the ICT business continuity plans taking into account the financial entity’s BIA and the ICT risk assessment. Financial entities shall assess through the testing of their ICT business continuity plans whether they are able to ensure the continuity of the financial entity’s critical or important functions. The testing of the ICT business continuity plan shall:

be performed on the basis of test scenarios that simulate potential disruptions, including an adequate set of severe but plausible scenarios. The scenarios considered for the development of the business continuity plans shall always be included in the testing;

include the testing of ICT services provided by ICT third-parties service providers, where applicable. In testing the business continuity plans as regards ICT third-parties services, financial entities shall duly consider scenarios linked to insolvency or failures of the ICT-third party service provider or of political risks in the provider’s jurisdiction, where relevant;

include scenarios of switchover from primary ICT infrastructure to the redundant capacity, backups and redundant facilities. The testing shall verify whether at least critical or important functions can be operated appropriately, for a sufficient period of time and whether the normal functioning may be restored;
be designed to challenge the assumptions on which the business continuity plans rest, including governance arrangements and crisis communication plans

include procedures to verify the ability of the staff of financial entities, ICT third-party service providers, ICT systems and ICT services to respond adequately to the scenarios duly taken into account
Test results shall be documented and any identified deficiencies resulting from the tests shall be analysed, addressed and reported to the management body.

3. As part of the ICT risk management framework referred to in Article 6(1), financial entities shall implement associated ICT response and recovery plans which, in the case of financial entities other than microenterprises, shall be subject to independent internal audit reviews.
Financial entities shall develop ICT response and recovery plans taking into account the results of the BIA. The ICT response and recovery plans shall:
specify the conditions prompting their activation, deactivation and any exceptions;

describe what actions shall be taken to ensure the availability, integrity, continuity and recovery of at least ICT systems and services supporting critical or important functions
 of the financial entities
be designed to meet the recovery objectives of the operations of the financial entities;

be documented and made available to the staff involved in their execution and be readily accessible in case of emergency. Financial entities shall clearly define roles and responsibilities to that extent;
provide for both short-term and long-term recovery options including partial systems recovery
lay down the objectives and the conditions to declare a successful execution of the plans.

The ICT response and recovery plans shall identify relevant scenarios, including scenarios of severe business disruptions and increased likelihood of occurrence of disruption.
 The response and recovery plans shall develop scenarios based on current information on threats and on lessons learned from previous occurrences of business disruptions. Financial entities shall duly take into account all of the following scenarios
(a) cyber-attacks and switchovers between the primary ICT infrastructure and the redundant capacity, backups and redundant facilities;

(b) scenarios in which the quality of the provision of a critical or important function deteriorates to an unacceptable level or fails, and duly consider the potential impact of the insolvency or other failures of any relevant ICT third-party service provider;
(c) partial or total failure of premises, including office and business premises, and data centres;
(d) substantial failure of ICT assets or of the communication infrastructure;
(e) the non-availability of a critical number of staff or staff members in charge of guaranteeing the continuity of operations;
(f) impact of climate change and environment degradation related events, natural disasters, pandemic, and physical attacks, including intrusions and terrorist attacks;
(g) insider attacks;

(h) political and social instability, including, where relevant, in the jurisdiction from where the ICT third-party service provider provides its services and the location where the data is stored and processed;
.
(i) widespread power outages
